corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: we propose prototypical networks for the problem of few-shot classification where a classifier must generalize to new classes not seen in the training set given only a small number of examples of each new class prototypical networks learn a metric space in which classification can be performed by computing distances to prototype representations of each class compared to recent approaches for few-shot learning they reflect a simpler inductive bias that is beneficial in this limited-data regime and achieve excellent results we provide an analysis showing that some simple design decisions can yield substantial improvements over recent approaches involving complicated architectural choices and meta-learning we further extend prototypical networks to zero-shot learning and achieve state-of-theart results on the cu-birds dataset body

top_candidates.sentence: A natural direction for future work is to utilize Bregman divergences other than squared Euclidean distance, corresponding to class-conditional distributions beyond spherical Gaussians.
Pseudocode to compute the loss J(φ) for a training episode is provided in Algorithm 1.
We perform zero-shot experiments on the 2011 version of the Caltech UCSD bird dataset (CUB-200 2011) .
Algorithm 1 Training episode loss computation for prototypical networks.

We propose prototypical networks for the problem of few-shot classification, where a classifier must generalize to new classes not seen in the training set, given only a small number of examples of each new class.
We provide an analysis showing that some simple design decisions can yield substantial improvements over recent approaches involving complicated architectural choices and meta-learning.
We further extend prototypical networks to zero-shot learning and achieve state-of-theart results on the CU-Birds dataset.

While the problem is quite difficult, it has been demonstrated that humans have the ability to perform even one-shot classification, where only a single example of each new class is given, with a high degree of accuracy .
Ravi and Larochelle take the episodic training idea further and propose a meta-learning approach to few-shot learning.
We find empirically that the choice of distance is vital, as Euclidean distance greatly outperforms the more commonly used cosine similarity.
Neighborhood Components Analysis (NCA) learns a Mahalanobis distance to maximize K-nearest-neighbor's (KNN) leave-one-out accuracy in the transformed space.

Large margin nearest neighbor (LMNN) classification also attempts to optimize KNN accuracy but does so using a hinge loss that encourages the local neighborhood of a point to contain other points with the same label.
It does this by encoding each point within a dataset, taking a sample mean, and applying a post-processing network to obtain an approximate posterior over the statistic vector.

Edwards and Storkey test their model for one-shot classification on the Omniglot dataset by considering each character to be a separate dataset and making predictions based on the class whose approximate posterior over the statistic vector has minimal KL-divergence from the posterior inferred by the test point.
For class meta-data we use the 312-dimensional continuous attribute vectors provided with the CUB dataset.
For this dataset we found it helpful to normalize the class prototypes (embedded attribute vectors) to be of unit length, since the attribute vectors come from a different domain than the images.
Table shows that we achieve state-of-the-art results by a large margin when compared to methods utilizing attributes as class meta-data.

The approach is far simpler and more efficient than recent meta-learning approaches, and produces state-of-the-art results even without sophisticated extensions developed for matching networks (although these can be applied to prototypical nets as well).
We further demonstrate how to generalize prototypical networks to the zero-shot setting, and achieve state-of-the-art results on the CUB-200 dataset.
We further demonstrate how to generalize prototypical networks to the zero-shot setting, and achieve state-of-the-art results on the CUB-200 dataset.

On several benchmark tasks, we achieve state-of-the-art performance.
